title: RabbitMQ
description: RabbitMQ and AMQP: exchanges, queues, acknowledgements, durability, dead-lettering, ordering and HA.
questions: Describe the AMQP messaging model used by RabbitMQ: what are producers, exchanges, queues and bindings, and why do producers publish to exchanges instead of directly to queues?
Compare the exchange types: direct, fanout, topic and headers. Give a concrete routing scenario where each one is the right choice, including how topic wildcards (`*` and `#`) work.
How do consumer acknowledgements work? Explain ack, nack and reject (with and without requeue), auto-ack vs manual ack, and what happens to unacknowledged messages when a consumer connection dies.
What does it take for a message to survive a broker restart? Explain durable queues, persistent messages and publisher confirms — and why each of the three is needed for reliable delivery.
What is consumer prefetch (`basic.qos`) and why is an unlimited prefetch dangerous? Explain how prefetch interacts with fair dispatch across multiple consumers on one queue.
Explain dead-letter exchanges: which events cause a message to be dead-lettered, and how would you build a delayed-retry mechanism using DLX plus per-queue or per-message TTL?
What ordering guarantees does RabbitMQ give? Explain how competing consumers, requeues and retries can reorder messages, and what you can do when strict ordering matters.

RabbitMQ gives at-least-once delivery at best. Explain why exactly-once is not achievable end-to-end, where duplicates come from, and how consumers should be designed (idempotency, deduplication) to cope.

Compare quorum queues with classic mirrored queues for high availability: how does each replicate, why were mirrored queues deprecated, and what trade-offs do quorum queues bring (Raft, memory, poison messages)?

What happens when a RabbitMQ queue grows faster than consumers can drain it? Explain memory/disk alarms and publisher flow control, and describe the levers you have (more consumers, prefetch tuning, TTL, max-length policies) to keep the system stable.

questions.question: Which statements about the AMQP model in RabbitMQ are true?
options: Producers publish messages to exchanges, never directly to queues
Queues receive messages from exchanges through bindings
The producer must know which queues will receive its message
Direct and topic exchanges route based on the message's routing key

questions.question: Which exchange type routes by pattern-matching the routing key with * and # wildcards?
options: Direct
Fanout
Topic
Headers

questions.question: Which statements about a fanout exchange are true?
options: It ignores the routing key entirely
It delivers each message to every queue bound to it
It filters messages based on header values
It distributes each message to exactly one bound queue, round-robin

questions.question: Which statements about consumer acknowledgements are true?
options: Unacknowledged messages are requeued when the consumer's connection dies
Auto-ack risks losing messages if the consumer crashes mid-processing
Rejecting or nacking with requeue=false can send the message to a dead-letter exchange
Sending an ack forces the message to be persisted to disk

questions.question: What is needed for a message to reliably survive a broker restart?
options: The queue must be declared durable
The message must be published with persistent delivery mode
The publisher should use publisher confirms to know the message was persisted
Declaring the exchange durable is sufficient on its own

questions.question: Which statements about consumer prefetch (basic.qos) are true?
options: It caps how many unacknowledged messages a consumer can hold
Unlimited prefetch lets one fast-reading consumer starve the others
Prefetch=1 gives fair dispatch across competing consumers
Prefetch sets the maximum length of the queue

questions.question: Which events cause a message to be dead-lettered to a DLX?
options: The consumer rejects or nacks it with requeue=false
Its TTL expires while it waits in the queue
The queue's max-length limit pushes it out
The consumer takes a long time to process it

questions.question: Which statements about ordering in RabbitMQ are true?
options: Requeues and retries can reorder messages within a queue
Competing consumers on one queue can finish processing out of order
RabbitMQ guarantees global ordering across all queues of an exchange
A single consumer with prefetch=1 on a single queue preserves processing order

questions.question: Why is exactly-once delivery not achievable end-to-end with RabbitMQ?
options: Publisher retries and consumer redeliveries can both create duplicates, and no transaction spans broker and consumer state
RabbitMQ has no acknowledgement mechanism at all
Queues cannot be made durable
The AMQP protocol forbids idempotent consumers

questions.question: Which statements about quorum queues are true?
options: They replicate via the Raft consensus algorithm
They are the recommended replacement for deprecated classic mirrored queues
They support poison-message handling via a delivery limit (x-delivery-limit)
They are non-durable by design
